claimed_contribution: The paper claims to be the first study to examine how moment-to-moment entertainment value (EV) and information value (IV) affect consumers' decisions to continue or discontinue viewing TV commercials.
It claims to identify both independent and joint (multiplicative) effects of EV and IV levels and velocities on stopping behavior.

It proposes a methodological contribution by combining functional data analysis for multiple moment-to-moment content traces with a random-effects discrete-time hazard model that accounts for heterogeneity across consumers and commercials.
context: The empirical setting is television advertising, using commercials aired on Dutch television. Consumers could stop a commercial during laboratory viewing sessions, a behavior the authors note is closest to skipping and partly overlaps with zapping and zipping.
design_and_data: The authors report two laboratory experiments on TV commercial viewing behavior. Experiment 1 used 18 commercials and 71 consumers, and Experiment 2 used 27 commercials and 119 consumers; order was randomized, and the experiments differed in ad context (with versus without surrounding program material) and familiarity measurement. Independent samples of trained judges provided second-by-second EV and IV ratings (typically 20 judges per measure), plus overall uniqueness and familiarity controls. The analysis first built smoothed, weighted EV/IV traces and first derivatives via functional data analysis, then estimated random-effects discrete-time hazard models of the per-second probability of stopping viewing with time-varying EV/IV level and velocity terms and interactions.
main_findings: Higher moment-to-moment EV levels decreased the likelihood of stopping a commercial, while higher IV levels increased it, in both experiments.
EV and IV levels showed a positive interaction: the probability of stopping was highest when EV and IV were simultaneously high, indicating conflict rather than compensation at high levels.

EV velocity reduced stopping, IV velocity increased stopping, and the EV velocity x IV velocity interaction was positive; the full moment-to-moment model outperformed benchmark models based on averaged or raw summary content measures.
one_line_summary: Across two lab experiments, the study finds that second-by-second entertainment keeps viewers watching TV commercials, information pushes them to stop, and high or rising entertainment and information together most strongly increase stopping risk.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior advertising work on overall feelings-versus-facts effects on viewing time by showing that second-by-second EV and IV levels, changes, and interactions predict real-time stopping decisions during commercials.
research_gap: Prior research had mainly examined overall commercial features and consumer characteristics, leaving little evidence on how moment-to-moment ad content affects decisions to continue or discontinue viewing during exposure. The paper also identifies scarce evidence on the joint dynamic effects of entertainment and information within commercials.
research_question: How do moment-to-moment entertainment value and information value in TV commercials influence consumers' second-by-second decisions to continue or stop viewing? Do the levels and velocities of EV and IV have independent and joint effects on stopping probability?
